ontology.foodon_gap_note: No exact FoodOn term for egg roll biscuit; mapped to closest baked cookie/wafer category. FOODON_03301710 (cookie) is the nearest defensible anchor.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A00LC covers biscuits and cookies broadly; no specific egg roll biscuit sub-code exists. Closest parent match used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT code identified for egg roll biscuit; omitted to preserve ontology honesty.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving is one egg roll biscuit piece weighing approximately 10 g, providing about 53 kcal.

nutrient_source.title: Egg Roll Biscuit — Singapore HPB Foodo Food Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values taken directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Egg Roll Biscuit (cr_id F11080025). All core macros and secondary nutrients are sourced from this record at confidence 0.95.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of Biscuits and Wafer Cookies — University of Sydney GI Database analogous estimate
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI entry exists for egg roll biscuit in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 70 is estimated from the closest analogous food category: plain sweet biscuits and wafer cookies, which consistently return GI values of 65–76 in the University of Sydney GI Database (e.g., plain sweet biscuit GI ~69–70, wafer GI ~70). GL per serving calculated as (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (70 × 5.628) / 100 = 3.94 for a 10 g serving.

hypertension.key_factor: Very high sugar content (33.98 g/100 g) with negligible dietary fibre (0.08 g/100 g), driving insulin-mediated sodium retention and elevated blood pressure risk independent of the low sodium content (72 mg/100 g).
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should avoid or strictly limit egg roll biscuits due to the very high sugar load (34 g/100 g), which promotes insulin-driven sodium retention and cardiovascular strain even though absolute sodium is low; if consumed, restrict to one piece occasionally and pair with high-fibre foods to blunt the glycaemic response.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Sugar at 33.98 g/100 g far exceeds the 20 g/100 g glycaemic ceiling, combined with a high GI of 70 and virtually no dietary fibre (0.08 g/100 g), producing a rapid and significant postprandial glucose spike.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Egg roll biscuits are unsuitable for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes due to extremely high sugar (34 g/100 g), high GI (70), and negligible fibre (0.08 g/100 g); they should be avoided entirely or, if consumed on rare occasions, limited to one small piece with blood glucose monitoring.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High total fat (29.91 g/100 g) with estimated saturated fat ~12.5 g/100 g and trace trans fat (~0.1 g/100 g), combined with very high sugar (33.98 g/100 g), raises LDL cholesterol and promotes atherogenic risk.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should avoid egg roll biscuits due to high saturated fat (~12.5 g/100 g), trace trans fat (~0.1 g/100 g), and very high sugar (34 g/100 g), all of which contribute to dyslipidaemia, endothelial dysfunction, and increased atherosclerotic risk.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Sugar at 34 g/100 g exceeds the 15 g/100 g RA threshold; saturated fat ~12.5 g/100 g exceeds the 5 g/100 g threshold; and trace trans fat (~0.1 g/100 g) is present — all three RA pro-inflammatory gates are triggered.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid egg roll biscuits entirely, as the combination of very high sugar, high saturated fat, and trace trans fat promotes systemic inflammation and may worsen joint symptoms.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Egg roll biscuit is a light, crispy, thin rolled wafer cookie popular in Singapore and across Chinese-influenced Southeast Asian communities. It is made by spreading a batter of eggs, wheat flour, sugar, and fat onto a hot iron mould, then rolling the freshly baked sheet into a tight cylinder while still warm, producing a delicate, hollow tube with a satisfying crunch. The flavour is mildly sweet and eggy, with a buttery undertone. Texturally it is extremely crisp and airy, dissolving quickly on the palate. Nutritionally, egg roll biscuit is a calorie-dense snack at 527 kcal per 100 g, driven by high fat (29.91 g/100 g, of which approximately 12.5 g is saturated) and high carbohydrate (56.28 g/100 g, of which 33.98 g is sugar). Dietary fibre is negligible at 0.08 g/100 g. Sodium is relatively low at 72 mg/100 g. The high sugar content, high GI (estimated 70), and near-zero fibre make this food clinically unsuitable for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes, hypertension, or cardiovascular disease. It is commonly consumed as a festive snack during Chinese New Year in Singapore and Malaysia, and is also sold year-round in supermarkets and provision shops. Known in Chinese as 鸡蛋卷 (Ji Dan Juan), it is a culturally significant treat associated with celebration and gifting.
common_user_queries: What is an egg roll biscuit?
How many calories in an egg roll biscuit?
Is egg roll biscuit suitable for diabetics?
Is egg roll biscuit high in sugar?
Can I eat egg roll biscuit if I have high blood pressure?
Is egg roll biscuit the same as a wafer roll?
How much fat is in an egg roll biscuit?
What is the GI of egg roll biscuit?

confidence_reason: Core nutrient values are sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id F11080025) at confidence 0.95. The food is an exact match in the primary regional source. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database category for plain sweet biscuits and wafer cookies, which is the closest defensible approved-source match; this does not reduce overall confidence to medium as the nutrient data itself is exact.
notes: 1. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid profile, micronutrient panel, and moisture data for this specific egg roll biscuit product are not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry (F11080025) as of 2026-04-16. 2. calcium_mg is reported as 0.0 and iron_mg is reported as 0.0 in the HPB source; these are confirmed zero values per the approved source. 3. saturated_fat_g of 12.5 g/100 g is estimated from the total fat content (29.91 g/100 g) and typical fatty acid profiles of similar biscuit products in USDA FoodData Central (SR Legacy: Cookies, wafers, sugar, regular); this is an approximation and should be verified against product-specific analysis. 4. trans_fat_g of 0.1 g/100 g is an estimate based on typical trace trans fat levels in commercially produced biscuits using partially hydrogenated vegetable fats; not directly reported in the HPB source. 5. GI of 70 is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for plain sweet biscuits and wafer cookies; no direct GI measurement for egg roll biscuit was found in the approved GI database. 6. Glycemic load per serving calculated as (70 × 5.628 g carbohydrate per 10 g serving) / 100 = 3.94. 7. Multilingual names provided for Singaporean cuisine category: English, Chinese (simplified and traditional), Malay, Tamil, Hindi.
